questions.question: Explain the difference between register direct and register indirect addressing modes. Provide one x86 assembly example and one MIPS assembly example for each mode, including the effective address calculation.
keyPoints: Register direct: operand is in the register itself (e.g., MOV AX, BX; add $t0, $t1, $t2)
Register indirect: operand address is in the register (e.g., MOV AX, [BX]; lw $t0, 0($t1))
x86 examples: direct 'mov eax, ebx', indirect 'mov eax, [edi]' where EDI holds memory address
MIPS examples: direct 'addu $t0, $t1, $t2', indirect 'lw $t0, 0($t1)' where $t1 holds base address

questions.question: Draw and explain the instruction execution cycle (Fetch-Decode-Execute) for the MIPS store word instruction 'sw $t0, 16($t1)'. Specify which CPU registers and bus signals are activated in each phase.
keyPoints: Fetch: PC → MAR → Memory → IR; PC+4 → PC; IR holds the instruction
Decode: IR → Instruction decoder; Registers $t0 and $t1 values are read into A and B buses
Execute: ALU computes effective address by adding sign-extended offset (16) to base register ($t1)
Memory: Data from register $t0 is written to memory at computed address; MDR → Memory data write

questions.question: (a) Distinguish between Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated (Port-Mapped) I/O in terms of address space utilization, instruction sets used, and hardware implementation. (b) Write complete x86 assembly code (using 16-bit real mode syntax) to: (i) Read a byte from I/O port 0x60 (keyboard data register), (ii) Check if the value is greater than 0x80 (indicating key release), (iii) If true, store the value to memory location labeled 'key_release' at offset 0x100 in DS. (c) Explain why IN and OUT instructions require different bus cycles than the MOV instruction when accessing I/O devices.
keyPoints: Memory-mapped I/O: I/O devices use same address space as memory, any memory instruction can access I/O (e.g., MOV AX, [0x400]); Isolated I/O: separate address space, requires special IN/OUT instructions; hardware: memory-mapped needs address decoding for both RAM and devices
Code: IN AL, 60H; CMP AL, 80H; JA store_release; JMP end; store_release: MOV [0x0100], AL; end: ...

IN/OUT assert separate I/O read/write signals (IOR#, IOW#) on system bus rather than memory signals (MREQ#, MEMR#); uses address lines A0-A15 for port address in 8-bit mode; no data transfer between registers and memory occurs

questions.question: Consider the MIPS instruction: sw $t0, 0($t1). (a) Determine the binary machine code for this instruction assuming $t0 = $8 (register $t0) and $t1 = $9 (register $t1), with data value in $t0 = 0x12345678 and memory address in $t1 = 0x10010000. Show all fields (opcode, rs, rt, offset, funct). (b) If the data cache miss occurs and main memory is accessed with DRAM offering 100ns access time, calculate the total time for this store operation assuming 5 clock cycles for cache miss handling and a 200MHz clock. (c) Explain how the datapath components (ALU, Register File, Data Memory, Sign Extend) are utilized during this store instruction's Execute and Memory phases.
keyPoints: Machine code: opcode 101011 (2Bhex), rs=9 (01001), rt=8 (01000), offset=0 (0000 0000 0000); Binary: 101011 01001 01000 0000000000000000; Funct field not applicable for store (R-type uses funct, I-type uses immediate/offset)
Time calculation: 5 clock cycles for miss handling = 25ns; Memory access = 100ns; Total = 125ns or 62.5 clock cycles at 200MHz

Execute: ALU performs addition of $t1 contents and sign-extended offset (0), result = effective address; Memory: Address from ALU sent to Data Memory, data from $t0 register written to memory through data write bus
